metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (a new package-data file) must be created — `known_subcommands()` is file-driven, not a hand-maintained set, so the help text file is required for `rebar audit --help` and `rebar audit serve --help` to work.
findings.finding: The plan's CLI wiring description says to list 'audit' in '_help.py::known_subcommands', but known_subcommands() is NOT a hand-maintained set — it is auto-derived by enumerating *.txt files in src/rebar/_cli/help/. A separate help/audit.txt file must be created; without it, 'rebar audit --help' returns an error path ('unknown subcommand') and 'rebar audit serve --help' is unserved, even though the intercept itself works.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt' to the Scope section, noting it is required for the file-driven known_subcommands() mechanism (parallel to how all other subcommands in _cli/help/ provide their help text).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the CLI dispatch model: specify whether `audit` is a pure intercept (like `workflow` — added before the `known_subcommands()` gate, no `.txt` file, owns its own argparse subparser) or a dispatch-table entry (needs `audit.txt` in `help/`, registered in `_SECTION_CLASSES`-style dispatch), and remove the contradictory reference to `_help.py::known_subcommands` if the intercept model is chosen.
findings.finding: The plan says the subcommand is 'listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`', but tool-verified: `known_subcommands()` is entirely file-system driven (it enumerates `*.txt` files in `rebar/_cli/help/`) — there is NO static list to edit. Simultaneously, the plan says dispatch is registered 'mirroring the `_workflow`/`_llm` intercept', but intercepts (workflow, llm, review, etc.) are NOT in `known_subcommands()` and have NO `.txt` file. These two wiring descriptions are mutually exclusive mechanisms, and the plan gives both without resolving which applies to `audit serve`.
findings.location: CLI section: 'registered in the CLI dispatch … and listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`'
findings.suggested_fix: Use the intercept model (matching `_workflow`): add `if argv and argv[0] == 'audit': return _audit(argv[1:])` before the `known_subcommands()` gate in `main()`, implement `_audit()` in a new `_cli/_audit_commands.py` with an argparse subparser for `serve`, and do NOT add an `audit.txt` file or touch `known_subcommands`. Remove the phrase 'listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`' from the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the premise in the Scope section: a `[ui]` section absent from `_SECTIONS` emits a WARNING (not a silent drop), so partial wiring is detectable in tests; update the rationale to reflect the actual `coerce_sparse` behavior.
findings.finding: The plan states a `[ui]` section absent from `_SECTIONS` 'is silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`', but `coerce_sparse` actually WARNS on unknown sections (line 591: `logger.warning(…)`) and hard-errors with `strict=True`. This mischaracterises the existing behavior and may mislead implementers about what happens before the wiring is complete (e.g., during an incremental rollout where only some of the 4 edits are applied).
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — the FULL `UiConfig` wiring (a partial edit is silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`' with 'logged as an unknown-section WARNING by `coerce_sparse`', and note that partial wiring will produce warning noise in tests that assert clean config loading.

findings.finding: AC1's oracle ('a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server') does not verify the second half of the AC's own requirement: that the refusal message 'names the flag'. The message-content assertion is stated in prose but absent from the oracle.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC1 — 'refuses to start with a message naming the flag. Oracle: a test asserting … the disabled path does not start a server'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the oracle: 'a test asserting no fastapi module is imported by `import rebar`, the disabled path does not start a server, AND the stderr/stdout message contains "[ui] enabled".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that `config.py` re-exports every `_config_schema` class (it does not — `IdentityConfig` is absent) and explicitly call out the required `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` line in the `config.py` edit.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/config.py` re-exports every `_config_schema` class with an explicit `from rebar._config_schema import XConfig as XConfig` line. The plan says to re-export `UiConfig` from `config.py` but does not enumerate this as a concrete line-level edit, and the module has 12 explicit re-export lines already established — `UiConfig` must be added as a 13th. The plan's vague phrasing 'this module re-exports every `_config_schema` class' may lead an implementer to believe the re-export is automatic.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope: `src/rebar/config.py` — add the exact line `from rebar._config_schema import UiConfig as UiConfig` to the edit description, and remove the parenthetical '(this module re-exports every _config_schema class)' which is inaccurate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or cite by exact module+function) the 'read layer from story E' interface that `server.py`'s index route will call, so the AC #5 oracle test can be written against a concrete API contract.
findings.finding: The plan's `server.py` scope and AC #5 both reference 'the read layer from story E' as the data source for the index route, but no interface contract (module path, function signature, or return schema) for that layer is defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot write the index route or the AC #5 end-to-end test without knowing what this layer exposes.
Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies `docs/config.md` documents the `[ui] enabled` key (e.g. 'grep -q `ui.enabled` docs/config.md exits 0').
findings.finding: The scope bullet 'document the key in docs/config.md' (under `src/rebar/config.py`) describes a deliverable with no corresponding acceptance criterion, leaving it unverified — it could be skipped without failing any AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '`docs/config.md` documents the `[ui] enabled` flag and the `REBAR_UI_ENABLED` env override. Oracle: `grep -c "ui.enabled" docs/config.md` returns ≥ 1.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the story E read-layer dependency explicitly: name its module path and the function signature the index route will call (e.g. `from rebar.audit.store import list_audited_tickets() -> list[str]`), and either declare story E as a blocking prerequisite or provide an interim stub contract the executor can code against now.
findings.finding: The server.py scope references 'the read layer from story E' and AC 5 requires listing 'a seeded audited ticket' via that layer, but no interface contract for the read layer is stated and no formal dependency edge to story E is declared. An executor implementing the index route cannot know what function to call, and AC 5 cannot be verified if story E has not shipped (VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT #9: sibling-ticket files referenced without a dependency edge).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Dependencies' section: 'Depends on story E (audit read layer). Expected interface: `rebar.audit.store.list_audited_tickets() -> list[TicketSummary]`. If story E is not yet merged, implement the index route against a stub that returns an empty list and note the integration test as provisional.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the empty-state rendering for the index page (e.g., display a friendly 'No audit data found — run `rebar audit` first' message) and add an oracle to AC #5 or a separate AC asserting HTTP 200 with that message when no tickets are seeded.
findings.finding: The index page behavior when no tickets have audit data is unspecified — the plan and AC only describe the happy path (seeded audited ticket is listed) and leave the empty-state UX undefined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an empty-state variant to AC5: assert that the index returns HTTP 200 with an empty list (zero seeded tickets) and does not error, so the index page's behavior is tested for both the populated and unpopulated cases.
findings.finding: AC5 (the live end-to-end server test) only seeds a single audited ticket (happy path). The empty-state case — zero audited tickets — is not covered, leaving the index page's behavior with no audit data unspecified and untested. This is the sole UI behavior shipped in this story.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 oracle to: '(a) with one seeded audited ticket: HTTP 200 and the ticket appears in the listing; (b) with zero seeded tickets: HTTP 200 and the listing is empty (no server error or template crash).' A single parametrised test or a second fixture covers both.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the human/admin auth contract for the non-loopback (`--host`) path: either (a) name a minimum auth mechanism (e.g., a randomly-generated bearer token printed at startup, or HTTP basic auth), (b) make `--host` a hard error in this story with a 'not yet supported' message and defer the flag to a future story that adds auth, or (c) document the explicit no-auth rationale for non-loopback as an accepted risk with a named owner/decision record.
findings.finding: The plan ships a `--host` flag that allows binding to any interface (e.g., `--host 0.0.0.0`), making the service network-reachable beyond loopback. For this non-loopback path the plan explicitly defers human/admin authentication as 'OUT of scope — documented, not implemented,' but provides no description of what that documentation says, no blocking guard (only a stderr warning), and no minimum auth mechanism. Under T10-b2, a stood-up service that can be made untrusted-network-reachable without a specified human/admin auth mechanism is an under-specified contract.
findings.location: Section 'Endpoint access contract (human/admin auth)'; AC line 4 (`--host 0.0.0.0` warning); Scope bullet for `server.py`
findings.suggested_fix: For this story, reject non-loopback `--host` values with a `SystemExit` message: 'Non-loopback binding is not yet supported (no auth mechanism is implemented); bind to 127.0.0.1 or add auth before exposing to a network.' Defer the `--host` flag to a future story that pairs it with a startup-token or gateway-auth design. This removes the network-exposure path from scope without losing the loopback-only vertical slice goal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare an explicit access posture for the non-loopback `--host` path: either (a) refuse non-loopback binds with a hard error ("not supported in this release") making the flag a forward-compatible stub only, or (b) document it as 'public-with-rationale' (no auth, plaintext, single-tenant) AND add a prominent plan note that the deployer accepts responsibility for network-layer isolation — the current 'out of scope / warn only' leaves the access class undeclared, which T5c flags as a gap.
findings.finding: The `--host` flag is fully implemented (CLI spec, AC4 test) and allows binding to non-loopback addresses, but the plan declares no access posture for that boundary-crossing path — it defers auth as 'out of scope' while still shipping the mechanism. Audit data (ticket content, audit findings) would flow over plaintext HTTP to any network principal that can reach the host, with only a stderr warning as a guard. Per the T5c zero-trust caveat this is advisory (internal/single-tenant tool), but the undeclared access posture on the non-loopback path is the gap.
findings.location: 'Endpoint access contract' section; CLI spec `--host` flag; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: In the CLI handler, after the warning, add a guard: if the resolved host is not in ('127.0.0.1', '::1', 'localhost'), print the warning AND exit with a non-zero code and a message like 'Non-loopback binding is not supported in this release; use a reverse proxy with appropriate auth.' This makes --host a no-op stub today (the flag exists for future use) without silently allowing an unauthenticated network surface. Update AC4 to assert the non-zero exit rather than just the warning text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the bespoke `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit` in the CLI handler with `rebar._optional.guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')`, and register `'ui': ('fastapi', '...')` in `_optional.EXTRAS` — the established single-chokepoint pattern already tested and documented.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke bare `try/except ModuleNotFoundError` + `SystemExit` inside the CLI handler for the missing-extra case, while `rebar._optional.guard_import` already exists as 'the single chokepoint that turns a missing extra into ONE clear, actionable error naming the exact pip install.' This is NIH: two existing production call-sites use `guard_import` (`grounding/__init__.py`, `llm/evals/eval.py`), a dedicated test module covers it (`tests/unit/test_optional.py`), and the docstring explicitly forbids the pattern the plan chooses. The plan also omits registering `'ui'` in `_optional.EXTRAS` (required for `guard_import` to produce the install hint and for `extra_installed()` to work), which means even if the author intends to use `guard_import`, the wiring is incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: In _optional.EXTRAS add: `'ui': ('fastapi', 'the local audit web UI (read-only index of audited tickets)')`. In the CLI handler: `from rebar._optional import guard_import; guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` instead of the bare try/except.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py::INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` to the plan's scope: a new `"audit"` entry with a one-line description is required whenever the `if argv[0] == "audit"` arm is added to `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py::INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` is not in the plan's scope, but adding `if argv[0] == 'audit'` to the intercept ladder in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` will cause the CI drift gate (`gen_cli_reference.py --check`, run in both `gerrit-verify.yaml` and `test.yml`) to fail immediately because `ladder_intercepts()` parses all `argv[0] == "<name>"` patterns from source and asserts they exactly match the `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` — add `"audit": "Start the audit UI web server (requires `[ui]` extra and `[ui] enabled = true`)."` to `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Register `'ui'` in `_optional.py::EXTRAS` and replace the proposed bare `ModuleNotFoundError`+`SystemExit` in the CLI handler with `guard_import('fastapi', extra='ui')` / `require_extra('ui')`, consistent with the agents/eval/tracing/grounding pattern.
findings.finding: The plan proposes a bare `ModuleNotFoundError` catch + `SystemExit` in the CLI handler for the missing-extra case, reinventing the existing `guard_import` / `require_extra` / `OptionalDependencyError` mechanism in `src/rebar/_optional.py`, which already exists precisely for this purpose and is used by the agents, eval, tracing, and grounding extras.
findings.location: Scope section: 'New src/rebar/audit/server.py ... lazy-imported inside the CLI handler with a friendly "install nava-rebar[ui]" SystemExit on ModuleNotFoundError'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'ui': ('fastapi', 'the audit UI web server (read-only local ticket browser)')` to `EXTRAS` in `_optional.py`, then use `from rebar._optional import require_extra; require_extra('ui')` at the top of the `audit serve` CLI handler instead of the bare try/except.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly justify why `UiConfig` is placed in the core `Config` dataclass and `_SECTIONS`/`_SECTION_CLASSES` (full core registration) rather than following the `llm` precedent of `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` + optional-layer parsing, or adopt the `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` pattern for consistency.
findings.finding: The plan registers `ui` as a full core config section (in `_SECTIONS`, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, and `Config`) rather than as a `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` entry — the established pattern for optional-layer config (e.g., `llm` is in `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` and is parsed by `rebar.llm.LLMConfig.from_env`, keeping the stdlib core from importing the optional layer). The plan does not justify this architectural choice against the prior art.
findings.location: Scope section: 'src/rebar/_config_schema.py — the FULL UiConfig wiring ... (1) add a UiConfig dataclass ... (2) add ui: UiConfig ... to Config ... (3) register "ui": UiConfig in _SECTION_CLASSES; (4) add "ui": {...} to the _SECTIONS coercer map'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `jinja2` is a net-new dependency (not part of the existing reviewbot stack) and verify the FastAPI `Jinja2Templates` / `TemplateResponse` integration pattern in the plan's Approach Rationale or Scope — either by citing a concrete usage sketch or noting it as a new surface distinct from the reviewbot JSON-only stack.
findings.finding: The plan claims to 'reuse the reviewbot web stack' to justify adding jinja2 to the [ui] extra, but the reviewbot extra today contains only fastapi + uvicorn[standard] + boto3 (pyproject.toml line 93) — no HTML templating. Jinja2 is net-new to the repo (zero usage in src/), so the HTML-rendering surface via fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates is UNVERIFIED in this codebase and cannot be characterised as a reuse.
findings.location: Scope section — `pyproject.toml` bullet; Approach Rationale — 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach Rationale, change 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack' to 'Reuse FastAPI/uvicorn (already in the reviewbot extra); add jinja2 as a net-new dep for HTML templating via FastAPI's Jinja2Templates.' Add a brief note in the Scope bullet that the server.py will use `fastapi.templating.Jinja2Templates` with a templates/ subdirectory, so implementers know this surface needs explicit wiring.

findings.finding: The plan adds `rebar audit serve` to the CLI but does not name the two unenumerated consumers that will break CI if not updated: (a) `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` needs an intercept arm (`if argv[0] == "audit"`) for the new subcommand, and (b) `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py::INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` must gain an `"audit"` entry (the drift gate in `test_gen_cli_reference.py` / CI regenerates `docs/cli-reference.md` and fails the build if any intercept arm lacks a curated entry). Additionally, if `audit` is help-backed rather than intercept-style, a `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (or `audit-serve.txt`) is required for `known_subcommands()` to recognize it.
findings.location: Scope section / CLI `rebar audit serve` bullet / src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py / scripts/gen_cli_reference.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the loopback-only, no-auth posture (and its explicit out-of-scope network-exposure decision) in a durable location — ADR, CLAUDE.md, or `docs/architecture.md` — so the contract survives beyond this story's plan.
findings.finding: The no-auth loopback security posture is a novel architectural decision (justified by analogy to datasette/mlflow/git instaweb) but is not captured in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc — only inline prose in the plan itself.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step to `## Scope`: 'Update `docs/architecture.md` (or create `docs/adr/0NNN-audit-ui-auth-posture.md`) documenting the loopback-only, read-only, no-auth decision and the explicit out-of-scope boundary for network exposure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add error handling in `server.py`/the CLI handler for bind failures (`OSError` on startup) that emits an actionable message (e.g. 'Port 8765 is already in use; try --port <N>') and exits non-zero instead of printing a traceback.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for server startup failures (port already in use, OS-level bind permission errors), leaving the operator with an unhandled exception rather than an actionable message.
findings.location: New `src/rebar/audit/server.py` scope item; AC line 5 (live end-to-end test)
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `uvicorn.run(...)` call in a `try/except OSError as e` block in the CLI handler; catch the bind error and print a human-readable message with the suggested `--port` flag before calling `sys.exit(1)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified error-handling path for EADDRINUSE: `server.py` should catch the bind error and exit with an actionable message (e.g., 'Port 8765 is already in use — try --port <other>') rather than propagating a raw traceback; add a corresponding AC or extend AC #5's oracle.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify behavior when the default port (8765) is already bound — users will see a raw uvicorn/OS traceback rather than an actionable error message.
findings.suggested_fix: In `serve(host, port)`, wrap the `uvicorn.run(...)` call in a try/except for `OSError` (errno EADDRINUSE) and emit a formatted message to stderr before calling `sys.exit(1)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the shutdown/teardown path for the server: confirm uvicorn's default SIGINT/SIGTERM handling is sufficient, and specify how the live end-to-end test (AC #5) stops the server after assertions (e.g., a background thread with `server.should_exit`, a subprocess with `terminate()`, or a pytest fixture with explicit teardown).
findings.finding: The plan does not address the server's graceful shutdown path — there is no mention of signal handling, lifecycle hooks, or how the server is stopped cleanly (e.g., Ctrl+C, SIGTERM from a test harness).
findings.suggested_fix: Use `uvicorn.Server` with `server.should_exit = True` in a pytest fixture teardown, or run the server in a subprocess and call `proc.terminate()` after assertions — specify which pattern is expected in `server.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (or `audit-serve.txt`) package-data file so `known_subcommands()` recognises `audit` (or `audit-serve`) and the `main()` gate does not reject it before `_dispatch` is reached; update the scope to name this file explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan states 'listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`' as if that function has a static list to edit, but `known_subcommands()` is ENTIRELY driven by `.txt` files present in the `rebar/_cli/help/` package-data directory — there is no in-code list in `_help.py`. Without a new `audit.txt` (or `audit-serve.txt`) file in that directory, the `if sub not in _help.known_subcommands()` gate in `main()` will reject `rebar audit serve` as an unknown subcommand before `_dispatch` is ever reached.
findings.location: CLI section: 'CLI `rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port 8765]` — registered in the CLI dispatch (`src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main`…) and listed in `_help.py::known_subcommands`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (and/or `audit-serve.txt`) to the Scope section as a required deliverable, since `known_subcommands()` is purely file-driven from that directory. The CLI intercept in `main()` must also be added (that part is already described), but without the `.txt` file the subcommand is unreachable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` (the pinned usage text for `rebar audit serve [--host HOST] [--port PORT]`) to the plan's scope, or explicitly document that `audit` is an intercept-arm command with no `help/*.txt` (matching `enrich`, `explain`, etc.).
findings.finding: The plan says to list `audit serve` in `_help.py::known_subcommands` but does not name the required `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` package-data file. `known_subcommands()` is derived exclusively from `help/*.txt` filenames; no `.txt` file = `"audit"` never appears in `known_subcommands()`, and `rebar audit --help` returns exit 1 with an 'unknown subcommand' error rather than usage text.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `src/rebar/_cli/help/audit.txt` to the Scope or clarify that `audit` follows the intercept-arm pattern (owns its own `--help` handler, no `help/*.txt`, appears in `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` instead of `known_subcommands()`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the extra composition strategy: either (a) extend `reviewbot` to include `jinja2` and make `ui` depend on it, or (b) explicitly justify why a separate `ui` extra duplicating fastapi+uvicorn is preferable to composing with `reviewbot`, and correct the 'reusing the reviewbot stack' framing to accurately reflect that jinja2 is net-new.
findings.finding: The plan asserts it is 'reusing the reviewbot web stack' and proposes `[ui] = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]`, but the existing `reviewbot` extra is `["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "boto3"]` — jinja2 is NOT part of the reviewbot stack. The plan does not address whether jinja2 should be added to `reviewbot`, whether `ui` should depend on `reviewbot`, or why a separate extra duplicating fastapi+uvicorn rather than composing with/extending the existing extra is the right choice.
findings.location: Scope section: 'pyproject.toml — add [project.optional-dependencies] ui = ["fastapi", "uvicorn[standard]", "jinja2"]  (reusing the reviewbot stack)'; Why section: 'Reuse the reviewbot FastAPI/uvicorn stack'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the two-word `rebar audit serve` command is dispatched: either (a) treat `audit` as a namespace intercept at `argv[0]` with an inner router (documenting this as a new pattern), or (b) rename to flat `rebar audit-serve` consistent with the established `review-plan` / `verify-completion` / `jira-onboard` convention.
findings.finding: `rebar audit serve` introduces a two-word subcommand namespace (`audit` as a grouping prefix, `serve` as the action), which is novel relative to every existing subcommand in the codebase (all use flat, hyphenated names like `review-plan`, `verify-completion`, `jira-onboard`). The dispatch architecture in `_cli/__init__.py` routes on `argv[0]` only. The plan does not discuss how `audit serve` is parsed or dispatched, nor does it justify the two-word structure against the established flat-name convention.
findings.location: Scope section: 'CLI rebar audit serve [--host 127.0.0.1] [--port 8765] — registered in the CLI dispatch (src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py::main, mirroring the _workflow/_llm intercept)'

findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` as the only edit target for `UiConfig`, but the existing wiring pattern requires three additional edits in the same file (`_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS` coercer map, `Config` dataclass field), plus a re-export in `src/rebar/config.py` (which re-exports every `_config_schema` class). Without these, `[ui] enabled` would be silently dropped as an unknown section by `coerce_sparse`. None of these four mandatory co-edits appear in the plan's Scope or file_impact list.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, extend the _config_schema.py bullet to list: add `UiConfig` dataclass; add `ui: UiConfig = field(default_factory=UiConfig)` to `Config`; add `"ui": UiConfig` to `_SECTION_CLASSES`; add `"ui": {"enabled": lambda v, k: _as_bool(v, k)}` to `_SECTIONS`. Add `src/rebar/config.py` to file_impact with reason 're-export UiConfig'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
